name: Platform-Neutral fallback requirements in AltID procurement
consequences: Pulling this lever tightens procurement terms to demand platform-neutral fallback authentication interfaces and standards-based anchor signals, reducing reliance on Apple/Google for core public access and creating a defined exit path if platform policies change. Downstream, procurement timelines may lengthen and incumbents may resist, while the public sector must invest in standards development, interoperability testing, and certification to avoid gaps during transition.
options: Mandate AltID procurement contracts to specify platform-neutral fallback authentication interfaces and standards-based anchor signals aligned with NSIS and eIDAS.
Require multi-source authentication anchors and open APIs in all AltID-related tenders to prevent lock-in to Apple or Google signals.
Establish a buy-in plan that phases in fallback-path requirements alongside existing MitID dependencies to test trade-offs before production.
review: Core tension: pushing platform-neutral procurement runs against incumbent control of platform signals; weakness: the options do not explicitly address regulatory or bureaucratic barriers that slow contract changes.

name: Credible, standards-based demonstrators for fallback paths
consequences: Direct effect: demonstrators provide tangible technical proof and risk insights; downstream: the results may be hard to translate into policy commitments or procurement language if governance lags.
options: Develop a mobile demonstrator that uses GrapheneOS and FIDO2/WebAuthn with cross-platform credential sharing to show platform independence.
Create a browser-based fallback demonstrator using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect with hardware-token backup for redundant authentication.
Engage independent security researchers to audit demonstrators and publish open reports to raise policy credibility.
review: Core tension: demonstrators can persuade policymakers but may not scale; weakness: the options do not explicitly address certification processes or integration into the AltID roadmap.

name: Policy advocacy and coalition-building for resilience
consequences: Direct effect: creates political will and stakeholder pressure; downstream: risk of coalition fatigue or dilution if not tightly scoped.
options: Launch a cross-sector coalition and submit a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen emphasizing resilience and supplier diversification.
Publish a risk briefing for civil society and tech academia highlighting continuity-of-service gaps if platform dependence persists.
Organize a parliamentary roundtable with digital policy committee members to test acceptance of platform-neutral procurement language.
review: Core tension: coalition-building can dilute accountability; weakness: options do not specify decision rights or success criteria within the advocacy group.

name: EU and standards pressure for platform-neutral identity
consequences: Direct effect: aligns with European requirements and can unlock EU funding; downstream: risk of divergent national sovereignty concerns or slow EU policy timelines.
options: Engage EU bodies to incorporate platform-neutral access requirements into European Digital Identity Wallet materials at scale.
Develop a formal standards-compliance blueprint detailing required interfaces and credential workflows for fallback paths across public sector interoperability.
Draft procurement language templates that reflect NSIS, eIDAS, and wallet interoperability expectations for national-scale use.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can slow national reform; weakness: options do not explicitly address domestic procurement timing or budget constraints.

name: Continuity planning and risk management integration
consequences: Direct effect: formalizes risk management for identity access; downstream: resource burden and potential for overconfidence if drills are not aligned with real-world events.
options: Commission a formal continuity risk assessment of public identity workflows under platform outages and policy changes.
Institute incident-response drills that simulate platform integrity API disruptions and trigger fallback authentication scenarios for resilience.
Publish a contingency scenario dossier with triggers, thresholds, and decision gates for switching trust anchors.
review: Core tension: drills may create a false sense of security; weakness: options do not specify integration with real incident response teams or data-sharing constraints.

name: Modular architecture exploring trust anchors and interoperable signals
consequences: Direct effect: creates a technical path for decoupling MitID signals from platform dependencies; downstream: architectural complexity and governance overhead as multiple anchors require ongoing coordination.
options: Map an architecture that decouples MitID signals from device integrity APIs while enabling a pluggable trust-anchor layer.
Prototype a cross-platform authenticator layer supporting iOS, Android, and GrapheneOS with a unified API for fallback scenarios.
Document a risk register and decision framework for selecting platform-neutral technologies over proprietary signals in public procurement.
review: Core tension: modular trust anchors raise governance overhead; weakness: options do not specify a clear transition path, maturity thresholds, or vendor coordination requirements.

name: Fallback authentication standards charter integrated into AltID planning
consequences: Formalizing a standards charter steers AltID planning toward cross-platform authentication signals and clearly defined testable fallback scenarios. This creates a durable reference for procurement and regulatory review, but may slow near-term decisions as consensus is built and embedded in contracts. Downstream, it increases resilience by prioritizing interoperability, while risking delays if stakeholders resist changes to existing templates.
options: Codify a standards charter that mandates open, cross-platform authentication signals and defines testable fallback scenarios across AltID components.
Embed fallback-path requirements directly into Phase two demonstrator criteria to ensure measurable compliance before Phase three.
Establish a triage process for conflicting platform terms that temporarily blocks procurement until consensus on platform-neutral criteria is achieved.
review: Core tension lies in balancing ambitious platform-neutral criteria with the risk of stalling procurement; the options miss a clear funding or enforcement mechanism to compel adherence if parties diverge.

name: Open interoperability testbed for identity resilience
consequences: Creating an open testbed accelerates credible demonstrations of platform-neutral paths and invites diverse participants to contribute, increasing transparency. A potential downside is ongoing governance overhead and potential scope creep beyond the project’s non-production intent. The testbed can become a collaborative backbone for standards validation and risk disclosure, but requires sustained funding and governance to remain credible.
options: Launch an open interoperability testbed and invite both public and private participants to contribute reference implementations.
Run quarterly challenges focused on identity fallback paths using open standards like WebAuthn and OpenID Connect.
Sponsor a privacy-preserving demonstration using hardware tokens and GrapheneOS to show non-Apple/Google trust anchors in a controlled environment.
review: The core tension is balancing broad participation with credible, independent evaluation; options may underprovide a mandate for actionable outcomes or a funding mechanism to sustain the testbed.

name: Public-sector procurement reform to de-risk supplier lock-in
consequences: Reforming procurement reduces dependency on a single vendor paradigm and creates room for platform-neutral suppliers to compete. The trade-off is longer procurement cycles and potential political pushback from incumbent operators. Overall, reform improves resilience by embedding platform-neutrality into purchase criteria and lifecycle oversight.
options: Institute phased procurement criteria that favor platform-neutral fallback capabilities and equally weighted non-platform-specific risk assessments.
Create a reserved-path procurement track for platform-neutral solutions with independent evaluation to ensure transparent competition and rigorous assessment.
Implement a sunset clause on platform-dependence terms to require annual procurement reviews of supplier concentration and risk exposure.
review: Core tension is balancing speed with thorough evaluation; options may not specify how to fund reserves or how to handle transitional costs for incumbents.

name: EU-aligned endorsement and cross-border standardization engagement
consequences: Engaging EU bodies tightens alignment with NSIS/eIDAS2 timelines and creates external pressure for platform-neutral paths, reducing unilateral policy risk. The risk is the slow tempo of multi-country consensus and the possibility of diluted national preferences. The lever strengthens legitimacy and access to broader interoperability markets.
options: Establish a formal liaison with EU bodies to influence wallet interoperability requirements for platform-neutral paths.
Coordinate with other member states to publish joint policy notes on continuity, resilience, and platform-neutral access strategies across digital identity ecosystems.
Draft a compliance roadmap mapping Danish fallback concepts to NSIS and eIDAS2 anticipated timelines for implementation and testing.
review: The core tension is reconciling national objectives with EU timelines; options may overstate immediate leverage without aligned national resources or negotiated commitments.

name: Independent assurance lab for platform-neutral signals
consequences: An independent lab provides credible validation of fallback concepts and signals integrity, boosting trust in non-Apple/Google paths. The trade-off is the need for ongoing funding, governance, and potential scope disputes over certification criteria. It creates a neutral benchmark for procurement decisions and public accountability.
options: Set up an independent certification facility to validate fallback authentication against platform-neutral criteria.
Develop a threat-model suite focusing on platform-signals integrity, supply-chain risk, and incident response under shutdown scenarios.
Publish annual assessment reports with anonymized risk indicators to inform procurement decisions and to benchmark progress against resilience targets.
review: Core tension centers on sustaining an independent lab with funding and authority; options may not specify the integration points with procurement or oversight bodies.

name: Coalition-driven advocacy and governance framework for resilience
consequences: A formal coalition amplifies resilience framing, aligns stakeholders around a common procurement and standardization agenda, and helps move policy forward. The weakness is potential governance fragmentation or competing agendas among coalition members. Still, it provides legitimacy and political capital for platform-neutral objectives.
options: Create a formal multi-stakeholder governance board to oversee roadmap, milestones, and procurement alignment.
Host a policy-briefing series with Folketinget, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, and privacy groups to codify platform-neutral language into procurement rules.
Launch a shared risk registry documenting supplier concentration risks and mitigation actions, with public visibility and review cadence.
review: The core tension is ensuring cohesive direction across diverse members; options may not fully resolve funding or decision-authority disputes within the coalition.

name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication compliance in AltID procurement
consequences: Introducing formal platform-neutral fallback requirements directly changes procurement leverage: AltID contracts must accommodate non-Apple/Google trust paths and independent verification. This raises resilience by ensuring an alternate authentication route is supported; it also increases integration complexity, requires new governance and testing work, and can extend procurement timelines while standards settle.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into AltID procurement criteria and audit processes to ensure ongoing supplier neutrality throughout the contract lifecycle.
Adopt a standards-based fallback pathway with cross-vendor attestations and independent certification to enable auditability without platform lock-in.
Publicly publish a phased rollout plan incorporating fallback readiness milestones and procurement gates to align supplier expectations with resilience objectives.
review: Core tension centers on accelerating procurement while guaranteeing fallback resilience; weakness the options miss: they do not specify funding for certification activities or governance cadence to sustain fallback readiness across multiple procurement cycles.

name: Open-source reference implementations for FIDO2/OpenID Connect in GrapheneOS environments
consequences: Direct effect: fosters verifiable, platform-neutral authentication paths that can be tested and audited. Downstream: attracts community contributions and accelerates standards adoption, but increases security review overhead and potential misalignment with procurement timelines.
options: Publish modular reference implementations for FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS and comparable platforms to demonstrate cross-platform compatibility.
Pilot a shared-authenticator stack leveraging hardware tokens and multi-signal attestation to reduce platform dependence.
Convene a cross-border code-review session with EU partners to harmonize interfaces and testing criteria for fallback paths.
review: Core tension centers on openness vs certification discipline; weakness the options miss: the funding, maintenance, and long-term governance of open-source artifacts.

name: Regulatory risk mapping and governance-committee oversight for supplier diversification
consequences: Direct effect: creates formal mechanism to monitor regulatory obstacles and coordinate cross-ministry action; downstream: enhances legitimacy of platform-neutral strategy but costs governance overhead and could slow progress.
options: Establish a recurring regulatory risk briefings and a formal governance body with cross-ministerial representation to steer platform-diversification strategies.
Institute a risk-adjusted procurement pathway that prioritizes contracts with proven interoperability, even if margins are higher.
Commission external regulatory impact assessments to quantify policy obstacles and secure feedback loops into AltID planning.
review: Core tension: governance speed vs thorough risk analysis; weakness: options don't specify how to fund or enforce any new governance body.

name: Multi-stakeholder scenario planning and red-team security exercises for resilience claims
consequences: Direct effect: builds credibility by stress-testing fallback models; downstream: requires substantial resources and can surface political or operational tensions that slow decision-making and push timelines.
options: Schedule quarterly red-team exercises simulating platform-swap events to validate fallback pathways and incident response.
Develop scenario-driven decision trees linking procurement choices to service continuity outcomes for different geopolitical shocks.
Create a public-facing resilience dashboard summarizing exercise findings and remediation timelines to build trust while avoiding over-claiming.
review: Core tension: demonstration vs operational realism; weakness: options miss how to integrate findings into procurement planning and budget cycles.

name: Alternate distribution pathways validation through hardware tokens and browser-based fallbacks
consequences: Direct effect: demonstrates non-platform confinement distribution; downstream: may trigger regulatory, privacy, or user-experience concerns and requires investment in hardware/token ecosystems.
options: Test browser-based fallback authentication using secure WebAuthn flows with token-backed attestation in controlled environments.
Pilot hardware-token deployment with offline verification for essential services to ensure continuity during platform outages.
Engage with regional distributors to validate availability of non-proprietary app-store channels and ensure compliant install pathways.
review: Core tension: user friction and policy alignment; weakness: options do not address how to secure funding or pilot adoption at scale.

name: EU interoperability alignment through active participation in European Digital Identity Wallet standards bodies
consequences: Direct effect: aligns national initiatives with EU interoperability goals and reduces cross-border friction; downstream: may require compromise on national preferences and lengthen decision cycles.
options: Register as a participant in European Identity Wallet working groups and contribute concrete Danish use-cases for platform-neutral access.
Draft a Danish position paper aligning national procurement language with EU interoperable standards to facilitate cross-border use.
Coordinate with EU pilots to test shared fallback models across member states and gather evidence for procurement alignment.
review: Core tension: national sovereignty in procurement vs EU harmonization; weakness: options may underplay risk of mis-timed EU cycles and funding constraints.
